title: Cat Head Pressing: Why Your Cat is Pressing Their Head Against the Wall
meta_desc: Is your cat pressing their head against the wall? Learn why head pressing is a veterinary emergency and what steps you need to take immediately.
content_html:
What is Head Pressing?

Head pressing is a distinct clinical sign where a cat compulsively and forcefully leans their forehead against a wall, a corner, or another hard object for an extended period. It is important to distinguish this from "head bunting," which is the affectionate behavior where a cat rubs their face against you or furniture. In head pressing, the cat remains still, often appears dazed, and does not seem to be seeking attention or comfort.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, head pressing is a critical veterinary emergency. It typically indicates a serious neurological issue or damage to the forebrain. Potential underlying causes range from toxic poisoning (such as lead or antifreeze) and metabolic disorders (like liver shunt or kidney failure) to brain tumors, strokes, or severe infections. Because these conditions can be life-threatening, you should transport your cat to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a quick 10-to-15-second video or a clear photo of your cat performing the behavior can be an invaluable tool for the veterinary team. Cats often experience a surge of adrenaline when they arrive at a clinic, which can cause them to temporarily stop the behavior or mask symptoms. Showing a recording allows the veterinarian to see exactly what is happening in the home environment, leading to a faster and more accurate diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is head pressing the same as head bunting?
faqs.answer: No. Head bunting is a social, affectionate behavior involving rubbing. Head pressing is a static, forceful push against an object and is a sign of neurological distress.

faqs.question: What are other signs that accompany head pressing?
faqs.answer: You may also notice your cat pacing, circling in one direction, having dilated pupils, or showing a sudden change in personality or vision.

faqs.question: Can I wait a few hours to see if it stops?
faqs.answer: No. Head pressing is a sign of significant brain or metabolic dysfunction. Immediate medical intervention is required to identify the cause and prevent further damage.
